did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Deep Residual Networks (ResNet50), for semantic segmentation and classification of Melanoma cancer using Dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset. The segmentation task achieved 96.6% accuracy, and classification tasks were performed on hybrid datasets including dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a pilot study proposing a Multi-Categorical Auto Diagnosis (M-CAD) system using Deep Learning (CNNs and ResNet50) for semantic segmentation and classification of Melanoma, Covid-19, and Carcinoma. It details specific methodologies, datasets (ISIC, CT-Scans), model architectures, training procedures, and quantitative results (accuracy, AUC), which are characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: M-CAD: Towards Multi-Categorical Auto Diagnosis of Varied Diseases using Deep Learning
journal: 2021 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Information Reuse and Integration for Data Science (IRI)
brief_summary: This study proposes a Multi-Categorical Auto Diagnosis (M-CAD) system using Deep Learning to simultaneously diagnose Melanoma, Covid-19, and various Carcinomas from Dermoscopy and CT-Scan images. The authors utilized a hybrid dataset comprising 1,279 Dermoscopy images from the ISIC dataset and 1,746 CT-Scans for a total of 3,025 images across eight classes. The methodology involved semantic segmentation of Melanoma lesions using a U-Net architecture and classification using both a custom CNN and ResNet50 models. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC, with the segmentation model achieving 96.6% accuracy compared to 90.9% for human experts. For classification, the ResNet50 model on masked Dermoscopic images achieved an accuracy of 80.2% and an AUC of 0.97 across all eight classes, demonstrating promising results for multi-disease diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Segmented using U-Net on ISIC Dermoscopy images (1279 images) and classified in hybrid datasets. Segmentation accuracy was 96.6%.
Diagnosed from CT-Scan images (746 images) as part of the multi-categorical classification task.

paper_terms: adenocarcinoma, large and squamous cell carcinoma

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the carcinoma types diagnosed from chest CT-Scan images (1000 images) in the hybrid dataset.
Used as a collective label for the carcinoma classes (adenocarcinoma, large cell, squamous cell) in the 4-class CT-Scan subset and the 8-class hybrid dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Covid-19, Adenocarcinoma, Large Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of 8 disease categories using Dermoscopy and CT-Scan images. Models include a custom CNN and ResNet50. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC. The primary task is disease diagnosis (classification).

all_task_types.target: Binary masks isolating Melanoma lesions from surrounding skin
all_task_types.details: Semantic segmentation of Melanoma lesions was performed using a U-Net architecture on Dermoscopy images. The output is a binary mask (lesion vs. skin). Accuracy was reported as 96.6%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dermoscopy Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) used for melanoma detection. The study utilized a subset of 1,279 dermoscopy images for classification and a superset of approximately 2,700 images for segmentation training/testing.

use.training: Used for semantic segmentation of melanoma lesions (2400 images) and as part of the hybrid dataset for classification (1279 images).
use.validation: Used for validation of the segmentation model (240 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification model as part of the hybrid dataset.
use.other: Approximately 2700 images were used for training and testing the segmentation model.

datasets_used.name: Covid-19 CT-Scan Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of chest CT-Scans used for Covid-19 detection. Combined with other datasets to form a hybrid multi-class classification dataset.

use.training: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for classification (746 images).
use.validation: Used as part of the hybrid dataset split for validation.
use.testing: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for testing.
use.other: Also tested in a specific experiment combining Covid-19 and Carcinoma CT-Scans.

datasets_used.name: Carcinoma CT-Scan Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of chest CT-Scans used for detecting various types of carcinoma (adenocarcinoma, large cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma). Combined with other datasets to form a hybrid multi-class classification dataset.

use.training: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for classification (1000 images).
use.validation: Used as part of the hybrid dataset split for validation.
use.testing: Used as part of the hybrid dataset for testing.
use.other: Also tested in a specific experiment combining Covid-19 and Carcinoma CT-Scans.

datasets_used.name: Hybrid Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom combined dataset created by the authors for multi-categorical auto-diagnosis, consisting of dermoscopy images and CT-Scans. It was shuffled and split 80% for training and 20% for validation.

use.training: Used for training the classification models (CNN and ResNet50) across different class configurations (e.g., 6 classes, 8 classes).
use.validation: Used for validation of the classification models (20% split).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification models (specifically 1961 images mentioned in Experiment 3 context, though total split was 80/20).
use.other: Used to evaluate the performance of the M-CAD system on varied diseases.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test the segmentation model on Dermoscopy images.
To normalize input images across each color channel to reduce problem complexity.
To monitor improvement in pixel-wise accuracy during segmentation training and stop if the model stops improving.
To dynamically optimize the learning rate during CNN training.
Used as the loss function for the regression model in segmentation.
To compare the segmentation accuracy of the proposed model (96.6%) against human experts (90.9%).
To compare the model's accuracy and AUC for Covid-19 detection and Carcinoma detection against previously published studies.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results were compared using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, AUC) and qualitative comparisons with human experts and literature values.

Segmentation (ISIC Dermoscopy Images).notes: Final segmentation accuracy reported in abstract. Tested on 10-fold cross-validation.

Classification (Single Class Detection).notes: Each disease variant predicted in different training sessions.

Classification (CT-Scan Only).notes: Dataset purely CT-Scan with both covid-19 and carcinoma as class labels. Abstract reports average accuracy of 81.5% and AUC of 0.94 for 6 classes using CT-Scans.

Classification (Hybrid Dataset - Unmaske.dataset: Hybrid Dataset (8 classes: Melanoma, Covid-19, Adenocarcinoma, Large Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
Classification (Hybrid Dataset - Unmaske.notes: Tested on 1961 total images. ResNet is front runner in most metrics.

Classification (Hybrid Dataset - Masked).dataset: Hybrid Dataset (8 classes: Melanoma, Covid-19, Adenocarcinoma, Large Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
Classification (Hybrid Dataset - Masked).notes: Masked Dermoscopic images showed significant improvement. ResNet50 achieved 80.2% accuracy and 0.97 AUC across all 8 classes. Proposed model had better AUC than ResNet in Exp 3A, but ResNet outperformed in Exp 3B metrics except potentially AUC where ResNet was 0.9706 vs CNN 0.9610.

architecture_used.main_model: Two architectures were used: a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classification and a U-Net architecture for semantic segmentation. For comparative classification, a Deep Residual Network (ResNet50) was also employed.
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50 was loaded from the Keras library (implies ImageNet pretraining, though not explicitly stated as 'ImageNet' in the text, it is standard for Keras ResNet50; however, the custom CNN was trained from scratch). The paper does not explicitly state pretraining details for the custom CNN or U-Net.
architecture_used.input_size: 128x128 pixels (for both segmentation and classification inputs). Input channels: 6 (RGB + HSV).

U-Net_Segmentation.structure: U-shaped encoder-decoder architecture. Encoder consists of 3 stages of convolution with pooling layers. Decoder consists of 3 stages of de-convolution with de-pooling layers. Skip connections concatenate symmetric layers from encoder to decoder.
U-Net_Segmentation.filters: Number of filters doubles at each subsequent stage in the encoder and halves in the decoder.
U-Net_Segmentation.activation: Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) for all layers except output.
U-Net_Segmentation.dropout: Applied after each stage of convolution and deconvolution.

Custom_CNN_Classification.structure: 3 stages of convolution with pooling layers, followed by 3 dense layers.
Custom_CNN_Classification.filters: Constant number of filters within each stage; number of filters doubles between subsequent stages (filters at end of stage 3 are 4x the first stage).
Custom_CNN_Classification.pooling: Pooling size of (2x2) after each convolution stage.
Custom_CNN_Classification.dense_layers: 3 dense layers with decreasing neuron size.
Custom_CNN_Classification.activation: ReLU for hidden layers, SoftMax for the multi-class output layer.
Custom_CNN_Classification.dropout: Added between every stage to limit overfitting.

epochs.U-Net: Maximum 1000 epochs (first experiment), 300 epochs (second experiment with augmentation). Early stopping used.
epochs.CNN/ResNet: Not explicitly stated, but early stopping was mentioned for segmentation.

data_augmentation.CNN/ResNet: Not explicitly detailed for classification, but hybrid dataset was shuffled.

training_details.normalization: Z-normalization across each color channel for input images. Ground truth masks normalized from 0-1.
training_details.validation: 10-fold cross-validation for segmentation. 80/20 split for classification training/validation.

classification_or_output_head.U-Net: De-convolution stages leading to binary mask output.
classification_or_output_head.Custom_CNN: SoftMax activation in the final dense layer for multi-class classification (8 classes in final experiment).
classification_or_output_head.ResNet50: Standard ResNet50 classification head (modified for 8 classes in final experiment).

preprocessing_techniques: Z-normalization across each color channel for input images
Normalization of ground truth masks from 0-1
Data augmentation via rotating and flipping original images
Use of HSV color space features in addition to RGB
Application of segmentation masks to dermoscopy images (masked vs un-masked)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to original Dermoscopy images during the second segmentation experiment to improve model accuracy from 94.4% to 95.2%. Specific probabilities or angles were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed Multi-Categorical Auto Diagnosis (M-CAD) system using Deep Learning is effective for simultaneously diagnosing varied diseases (Melanoma, Covid-19, and Carcinomas) from different medical image modalities (Dermoscopy and CT-Scans).
Semantic segmentation of Melanoma lesions using a U-Net architecture achieved an accuracy of 96.6%, outperforming human expert agreement which was reported at 90.9%.
Data augmentation techniques improved the segmentation model's accuracy by 0.8% (from 94.4% to 95.2%).
The classification model achieved an average accuracy of 81.5% and an AUC of 0.94 for 6 classes using only CT-Scans.
For the full 8-class hybrid dataset, the ResNet50 model applied to masked Dermoscopic images achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 80.2% and an AUC of 0.97.
Masked Dermoscopic images showed significant improvement in overall accuracy and metrics compared to un-masked images, primarily due to improved classification of Melanoma.
ResNet50 generally outperformed the custom CNN model in most metrics on the hybrid dataset, although the custom model showed better AUC in the un-masked experiment (Exp 3A).
The study establishes better accuracies and AUC values for Covid-19 detection (84.5% accuracy, 0.878 AUC) compared to previously mentioned literature (83.28% accuracy, 0.896 AUC).
Limitations acknowledged include the relatively small size of the publicly available datasets and the reduction of image resolution, which may remove important features for prediction.

Future work suggests expanding the number of diagnosable diseases, using ensemble approaches with Transformer Networks and Attention Mechanisms, and incorporating patient metadata to improve system performance.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study conducted on a small subset of publicly available datasets (total 3,025 images), which limits the generalizability and statistical power of the results.

The hybrid dataset combines disparate imaging modalities (Dermoscopy and CT-Scans) and disease types (Melanoma, Covid-19, Carcinomas) without apparent clinical relation, which may introduce domain shift issues not fully addressed.
Image resolution was reduced to 128x128 pixels for all inputs, which the authors acknowledge may remove important features useful for prediction.

The segmentation model (U-Net) was compared against human expert agreement (90.9%) rather than a rigorous multi-reader study, and the specific number of experts or their qualifications are not detailed.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on the specific hybrid dataset created by the authors, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific data distribution.

The classification task involves 8 classes with significant class imbalance (e.g., 1279 Melanoma vs 746 Covid-19 vs 1000 Carcinoma subtypes), yet class weights or balancing techniques are not explicitly detailed in the methodology.

The paper does not report confidence intervals or p-values for the reported accuracy and AUC metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the improvements over literature baselines.

Data leakage risk exists if the 'hybrid dataset' splitting (80/20) was not strictly stratified or if images from the same patient appeared in both training and validation sets, though patient-level splits are not confirmed.

The comparison with literature values for Covid-19 detection (84.5% vs 83.28%) is qualitative and lacks details on whether the baseline models were retrained or if the datasets were comparable in size and quality.

The system attempts to diagnose diseases from completely different anatomical regions (skin vs. chest) using a single unified architecture, which is a novel but potentially clinically impractical approach for a single deployment scenario.
